About

I'm Adam Penn, a founder, operator, and investor based in Northern California. I build businesses around real assets and run them with an engineering mindset: systems-first, data-driven, and built to last.

I run Bidwell Capital Fund, Penn Properties, and co-host the Wealth Independence Podcast.

The Longer Version

I studied Computer Science and started my career as a software engineer in Northern California. I enjoyed the craft: solving problems, building systems, shipping code. But somewhere along the way, I started to feel the pull toward something more tangible. I wanted to build things I could see and touch, not just deploy.

In 2017, I put $23,000 into my first short-term rental. I figured if I could debug software systems, I could figure out real estate operations. It turned out to be true, and more interesting than I expected. I used strategic debt and reinvested every gain. That one property became two, then five, then a $1.6 million portfolio, then a management company, then a fund.

Today I manage 35+ rental units through Penn Properties, where I handle everything from guest experience to maintenance systems to revenue optimization. On the investment side, Bidwell Capital Fund helps other investors access real asset strategies: short-term rental funds, oil syndications, and tax-advantaged structures.

The podcast started because I kept having the same conversations with friends in tech who wanted to invest but didn't know where to start beyond index funds. Dustin Bailey and I created Wealth Independence to share real frameworks, real case studies, and honest conversations about building passive income.

The common thread across everything I do is simple: I believe in owning real assets, running disciplined operations, and building toward a life where your work and your freedom aren't in conflict. That's the game I'm playing.

How I Think
  • I approach investing like an engineer: test the hypothesis, measure the result, iterate.
  • I'd rather own fewer things well than many things poorly.
  • Operations are the moat. Anyone can buy an asset; not everyone can run it.
  • I optimize for autonomy, not status.
  • I'm skeptical of complexity. The best systems are simple enough to survive stress.
